methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $36M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 25.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
ClearSpace, a Series A space-technology company founded by Luc Piguet, operates within a fragmented frontier-aerospace sector populated by 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 identifiable private peers including SpaceX and Sierra Space. The company has raised $36M to date.

Valuation triangulation is constrained by data limitations. Stage-progression methodology, the only fully applicable approach, extrapolates from ClearSpace's $36M cumulative raise using a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A progression to later rounds, yielding $72M. Public-comp anchoring cannot be deployed because the target's revenue is not disclosed—a common constraint for private firms—though the sector median P/S across public Space & Aerospace names stands at 25.8x. Peer-median methods failed to generate sufficient narrow-stage comparable density; while 11 same-sector private firms exist in the broader universe, filtering for same-stage or adjacent-stage resulted in fewer than three datapoints, rendering a defensible median unreliable.

Given single-method reliance, the analysis supports a valuation range of $61M–$83M, centered at $72M. This reflects conventional stage-progression mechanics rather than market-derived multiples. Primary uncertainties stem from the absence of revenue data (blocking public-comp methods), thin same-stage peer density in aerospace, and undisclosed prior-round terms that could anchor expectations differently. Future rounds should capture revenue trajectory and clearer margin profiles to enable multiples-based validation.
